name: Interactive Walkthroughs (Learn-by-Doing)

fields.definition_origin: The 'learn-by-doing' / 'practice round' onboarding pattern emerged from gaming UX in the 2000s. Pudding Monsters (ZeptoLab, 2012) is the canonical example cited in PDF Deep Dive — animated hand-overlay guides the user to perform the first puzzle move, then steps back. Predecessor patterns: Mario Bros (1985) world 1-1 design teaches mechanics through embodied play (Anna Anthropy, Dan Cook 'Game Design as Learning Theory' 2007 essays). For productivity: GitHub's 'Hello World' guided commit (2014+), Codecademy / Duolingo lesson architecture. NN/g categorizes as 'guided interactive onboarding'. DesignerUp 14 Types lists 'Interactive Tutorial' as a discrete category. Distinct from coach marks (item 22) — walkthroughs require the user to PERFORM the action, not just dismiss a hint. Distinct from full demos (Mau Baron's DEMO act, item 18) — walkthroughs are explicitly tutorial-coded with practice-round framing.
fields.core_mechanism: Operates via embodied learning (Lakoff & Johnson 1980; Glenberg et al. 2008) — physical action with the interface produces deeper procedural memory than passive observation. Mechanism stack: (1) procedural memory consolidation (basal ganglia + cerebellum) — performing an action retains better than reading instructions; (2) immediate feedback loop (Skinner 1938 operant conditioning) — successful action triggers reward signal, scaffolding next action; (3) low-stakes sandbox reduces failure anxiety (Csikszentmihalyi flow theory — challenge calibrated to skill); (4) IKEA effect — user 'completed' the tutorial, ownership of the technique forms; (5) competence satisfaction (Self-Determination Theory, item 5) — user feels capable, intrinsic motivation rises; (6) timing-of-instruction effect (Kalyuga 2007 expertise-reversal) — instruction-while-doing supersedes instruction-then-do for novices but reverses for experts; therefore walkthroughs MUST be skippable.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Procedural learning engages basal ganglia (caudate, putamen) and cerebellum. Mirror neuron systems (premotor cortex, inferior parietal lobule, Rizzolatti & Sinigaglia 2008) activate when user observes the demonstrated gesture. Reward feedback engages mesolimbic dopamine (Schultz 1997). Engagement-flow correlates with sustained alpha-band activity (Csikszentmihalyi+Nakamura). [uncertain on direct mobile-walkthrough fMRI]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Pudding Monsters (ZeptoLab 2012, PDF Deep Dive cite) — animated hand-overlay walkthrough on first level taught swipe-to-merge mechanic; reportedly cited internally as a key D1 retention lever for the studio's mobile titles. (2) Duolingo's first lesson is structurally a walkthrough — user performs a translation, gets immediate feedback, builds the lesson loop; Duolingo's D1 retention is sector-leading (~50%+) and the founder credits the immediate-action loop. (3) Mario 1-1 (Miyamoto, 1985) — landmark game-design study demonstrating embodied teaching without text; replicated as analytical framework in 100+ game UX papers. (4) Codecademy's first interactive lesson — user types Python, gets feedback in same screen; user enrolment and completion rates outperform text-tutorial competitors (industry benchmark). (5) Khan Academy interactive lessons — Stanford research (Koedinger 2012) shows interactive lessons outperform passive video by ~2x learning gain. (6) Mau Baron's DEMO act in story-onboarding (item 18) is a walkthrough variant — Headway, Headspace, Calm all use first-feature walkthroughs to drive D1 retention. (7) Snapchat's first-time camera tutorial (animated overlay teaching swipe-up filter) — high engagement on first session, cited in Snap design talks 2018-2021. (8) Mobbin teardowns of Duolingo, Codecademy, Khan Academy, Headspace, Calm confirm walkthrough patterns as 2024-2026 standard for skill-based and creative-tool apps.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Walkthrough completion rate (target ≥80%), first-action success rate (% of users who successfully perform the demonstrated action on first try), time-in-walkthrough (target 30-90s for single-action; 2-4min for multi-step skill walkthrough), Day-1 feature-reuse rate (does the user perform the action again unprompted within 24h?), D7 retention conditioned on walkthrough completion vs skipped.
fields.evidence_strength: industry_benchmark + case_study + practitioner_opinion (foundational learning research peer-reviewed; specific mobile-walkthrough A/B tests case-study level)
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Duolingo D1 retention ~50%+ (sector-leading, founder credits walkthrough loop). Stanford research on interactive lessons: ~2x learning gain over passive (Koedinger 2012, education context, not mobile-onboarding). Specific app-level A/B figures unverified at peer-reviewed RCT level for mobile.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Sandbox isolation: walkthroughs operate on disposable practice data, NOT user's real data — failure has no consequence. (2) Animated affordance: hand-overlay (Pudding Monsters), pulsing dot, animated arrow indicating the gesture/tap target — Lottie or Rive for the animation. (3) Wait-for-action: walkthrough does not progress until user performs the demonstrated action; provide gentle re-prompt after 5-10s of inactivity ('Tap the green button to try'). (4) Failure-tolerance: if user does the wrong action, gentle redirect ('Try tapping HERE instead') NOT punishment; do not reset progress. (5) Progressive complexity: first walkthrough teaches one action; subsequent contextual walkthroughs teach increments. (6) Skip affordance present but de-emphasized — power users can skip; novices proceed. (7) Single state-machine: walkthrough is a state in the FSM (item 30) with explicit entry / exit / step transitions. (8) Animated overlay layer: separate from the underlying UI so skip can simply hide overlay without unmounting product code. (9) Telemetry: emit `walkthrough_started`, `walkthrough_step_completed { step_id, attempt_count }`, `walkthrough_skipped { at_step }`, `walkthrough_completed { duration_ms }`. (10) For complex products (Figma, Linear): consider in-app sandbox 'Try it' button later in the user lifecycle (not just first-launch). (11) Real-product implementation (not stub): the walkthrough must use the REAL feature code, not a mocked simulation — otherwise users hit the production UI and re-encounter the friction.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Pudding Monsters (ZeptoLab 2012) — animated hand swipes across screen, user must replicate the gesture; canonical PDF cite. (2) Duolingo first lesson — translate a sentence with assists, immediate feedback, level-up celebration; the entire D1 funnel is built on this mechanic. (3) Headspace's 60-second breathing demo — within onboarding, user performs the actual breathing exercise (not watches a video). (4) Calm's first-time soundscape — user adjusts volume, picks a sound, plays it (active not passive). (5) Headway book-summary preview — user reads a 15-min summary live, scrolling and engaging. (6) Snapchat first camera tutorial — animated overlay teaches swipe-up filters, user performs the swipe. (7) Codecademy / Brilliant first interactive lesson — user types code/answer, gets feedback inline. (8) Procreate (iPad) first launch — guided interactive onboarding on canvas teaching brush, layers, gestures. (9) Notion first database tutorial — user adds a row and switches view in a guided sandbox. (10) Things 3 — Magic Plus button hint paired with first task creation; subtle walkthrough. (11) Mojo (Mau Baron alumni network) — 'Make your first reel' INSIDE onboarding before paywall. (12) Linear keyboard-shortcut hints surfacing as user does slow-path equivalent — reverse walkthrough. (13) Apple Vision Pro initial calibration — hand and eye tracking walkthrough.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile-primary for gesture-heavy walkthroughs (swipe, pinch, drag) — these don't exist on web. Mobile must use Lottie/Rive animated overlay; web uses CSS animations or Driver.js / Shepherd.js. Mobile walkthrough must coexist with native scroll/tap event handlers — careful overlay z-index management. Web equivalent: Codecademy / Brilliant interactive code editors are walkthrough-style. Mobile gesture walkthroughs require haptic feedback for confirmation (UIImpactFeedbackGenerator iOS, HapticFeedback Android).
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Liquid Glass (iOS 26+): translucent overlays for animated affordance work beautifully — semi-transparent hand overlay reveals UI beneath. M3 Expressive (Android 16+): expressive motion system (spring physics) is purpose-built for the affordance pulse and hand-glide animations. Both DLs reward physically-grounded motion (Apple's interface physics, Material's spring-based motion).
fields.category_fit: STRONGEST fit: gaming (Pudding Monsters, Mario, every game tutorial), creative tools (Procreate, Figma, Mojo, Capcut), edtech (Duolingo, Codecademy, Brilliant, Khan Academy), AI-creative tools (image gen, music gen — first creation must be guided), complex productivity (Notion databases, Linear cycles, Figma autolayout), AR/VR (camera walkthrough). Acceptable fit: wellness apps for first-meditation/exercise (Headspace, Calm, Headway). WEAK fit: utilities (overkill — frictionless wins, item 19), simple social apps, messaging (gestures are universal). EXCLUSION: 'Do not use a walkthrough for an action the user already knows (tap, scroll) — patronizing.'
fields.solo_dev_feasibility: 1_month. Building the walkthrough animation primitive is 1-2 weeks; sandbox isolation logic is 1 week; copy + animation authoring per walkthrough adds time. Lottie/Rive animation reduces art cost. For a single walkthrough, 1-2 weeks; for a multi-walkthrough product, a month.
fields.tooling_options: Lottie / Rive (animated affordance), React Native Reanimated 3 / SwiftUI / Compose Animation (overlay animation), Plotline (managed mobile walkthroughs), Appcues Mobile, Userpilot, Pendo. Game tutorials: Unity tutorial systems, Unreal blueprints. Web equivalent: Driver.js, Shepherd.js, Intro.js. Custom: 1-2 weeks of overlay engineering for solo dev.
fields.ai_dependency: none for the walkthrough itself. Optional: AI-personalized practice content (Duolingo's adaptive lessons, Brilliant's adaptive sequences) uses small classifier or recommender. State: none for static walkthrough; optional small classifier for adaptive walkthrough.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to build the walkthrough as an animated-overlay state in the FSM. Prompt fragment: 'Build a Walkthrough component that overlays the real product UI with an animated affordance (hand-overlay or pulsing-dot) indicating the next gesture. The walkthrough WAITS for the user to perform the demonstrated action — does NOT auto-advance. State machine: idle → demonstrating → waiting_for_action → success → next_step / done. Use Reanimated 3 spring animation (damping: 14, stiffness: 180) for the affordance pulse. Provide a `Skip walkthrough` tertiary text-link in the top-right (de-emphasized, not primary CTA). Sandbox isolation: walkthrough operates on disposable data, not user real data. Failure-tolerance: if user taps wrong target, redirect ("Try tapping the green button instead") without resetting progress. Wire telemetry: walkthrough_started / step_completed / skipped / completed.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'Mockup the first walkthrough screen of a creative app at 390x844. Background: the actual product canvas (image + tools panel) at full opacity. Foreground overlay: 50% translucent dim layer, with one element (the brush picker on the left edge) at full opacity (cutout). Animated hand graphic (peach skin tone, translucent, 96x96px) positioned over the brush picker, with arrow trail indicating a tap-and-drag gesture. Top: floating instruction card (white, 16px radius, 16px padding, fixed top inset 16), text: "Try drawing your first stroke — drag from here" (Cabinet Grotesk Semibold 17px). Top-right: small "Skip" tertiary text-link. NO modal blocking the canvas — overlay is partial. Forbid: blocking modal that prevents interaction, Lucide hand icon (use realistic hand illustration), full-opaque dim, Next/Previous tour-card navigation.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen typically generates a deck-of-cards 'tour' instead of a true interactive walkthrough — explicit PDF Deep Dive antipattern. Specific failures: (1) static modal cards saying 'Step 1: Tap here. Step 2: Drag there.' that user CLICKS NEXT through (no actual interaction with the demonstrated feature), (2) stub UI that simulates the feature instead of using real product code, (3) full-screen blocking dim that prevents user from actually doing the gesture, (4) auto-advancing tour that doesn't wait for user action, (5) no failure-tolerance — wrong tap resets the tour, (6) no skip affordance. Counter-prompt: 'CONSTRAINT: walkthrough is INTERACTIVE not a tour-card carousel. The walkthrough WAITS for the user to perform the demonstrated action; auto-advance is forbidden. The walkthrough operates on the REAL feature code in a sandbox isolation, not a stub. The dim overlay is PARTIAL (cutout around target) so user can interact with target. Skip affordance is mandatory. Failure-tolerance: wrong action redirects, does not reset. Forbid: deck-of-cards Next/Previous tour, full-opaque blocking modal, stubbed simulation UI, auto-advancing without action.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Deck-of-cards 'tour' carousel masquerading as walkthrough (canonical AI-codegen failure, PDF Deep Dive cite). (2) Stubbed simulation instead of real feature — user finishes 'walkthrough' and re-encounters the friction in real product. (3) Full-screen blocking modal that prevents the gesture from being performed. (4) Auto-advancing without user action (defeats embodied learning). (5) Mandatory walkthrough with no skip (No-Escape Room, Six-Antipattern #3). (6) Walkthrough on actions everyone knows (tap, scroll — patronizing). (7) Walkthrough operating on user real data — destructive. (8) Punishment on wrong action (resets, blocks progress). (9) Long multi-step walkthrough that exceeds working memory (>4-5 actions in one walkthrough). (10) Walkthrough as fix for unintuitive UI — should redesign UI instead.
fields.regulatory_risk: Low. Walkthroughs themselves are benign. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) flags 'manipulative consent UIs' but tutorial walkthroughs are explicitly permitted. Edge case: COPPA-K — under-13 walkthroughs must respect age-appropriate design (no targeted-advertising integration, no behavioral profiling for personalization). Walkthroughs that pressure paid feature use ('Try the premium brush!') edge toward dark-pattern. WCAG 2.2 alignment is the main risk.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple HIG: walkthroughs are explicitly endorsed when contextually placed and skippable. Apple Guideline 4.1 minimum functionality — walkthroughs are not a substitute for actual functionality. Material 3: walkthroughs in M3 should respect motion design system. Both: walkthroughs requiring sensor permissions (camera, mic, motion) must respect deferred permission patterns (item 24).
fields.accessibility_implications: WCAG 2.2: 2.5.7 Dragging Movements — gesture walkthroughs must provide tap alternative for motor-impaired users. 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions — animated affordance must respect prefers-reduced-motion (replace with static highlight). 1.3.5 Identify Input Purpose — walkthrough copy must describe expected input clearly. VoiceOver/TalkBack: animated hand has no semantic meaning — provide accessible label ('Practice tapping the brush picker'). Provide a non-gesture alternative path (button-based) for every gesture-based walkthrough. Dynamic Type 200%: instruction card must reflow.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Building real-product walkthroughs (not stubbed) is engineering-expensive — sandbox isolation is non-trivial. Solo devs often cut corners with stubs and lose the embodied-learning benefit. (2) Animation art is non-trivial cost (animated hand-overlay) — Lottie/Rive marketplace assets help but feel generic. Custom-illustrated animations cost money. (3) Localization of instruction copy must fit gesture context (RTL gesture metaphors differ — 'swipe right to next' in RTL means swipe LEFT). (4) Performance on low-end Android — animated overlays drop frames; provide reduced-motion fallback. (5) Walkthrough fatigue — multiple walkthroughs in one session = same problem as multiple coach marks. Limit to one significant walkthrough per session, drip subsequent ones across days. (6) Wrong-action handling difficult to test — extensive QA needed. (7) Server-driven walkthrough config is harder than for coach marks (animation references, sandbox states).
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Embodied learning is universal but gesture conventions differ. RTL: swipe directions and arrow indicators flip. JP/KR: walkthrough copy register more polite ('Please try tapping...'). MENA RTL: animated hand should mirror to match cultural expectation. CN super-app context: walkthroughs more accepted on dense UIs. Hand illustration skin tone: provide localized variants or use abstract glove/cursor in markets where skin-color representation is sensitive. Avoid: animated hand that gestures with culturally-inappropriate symbols (e.g., thumbs-up offensive in some MENA markets).
fields.prescribed_action: Use interactive walkthroughs for complex/novel feature introduction (gaming gestures, creative-tool workflows, first-meditation-style demos). Walkthrough must (a) operate on real product code in sandbox isolation, (b) wait for user action — do not auto-advance, (c) provide a skip affordance, (d) have failure-tolerance with gentle redirect on wrong action, (e) respect WCAG 2.2 alternatives for motor-impaired users. Forbid: deck-of-cards tour-card carousel, stubbed simulations, blocking modals, auto-advancing.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: N/A — walkthroughs are post-questionnaire. They live in the DEMO act of story-onboarding (item 18) or post-onboarding empty-state activation (item 21).
fields.aha_relevance: Direct — walkthroughs ARE the embodied Aha for skill-based / creative / complex apps. Duolingo's first lesson IS the activation moment. Mau Baron's DEMO act is a walkthrough variant. For these categories, walkthroughs replace AI Plan Reveal as the engineered peak.
fields.measurement_horizon: Day_1 (immediate retention conditioned on walkthrough completion) and Day_7 (sustained feature use)
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Magician (the walkthrough is the spell — first incantation), Hero (overcome the practice challenge), Sage (instructive scaffold), Caregiver (gentle scaffolded learning, failure-tolerance is care). Mismatches: Outlaw (mandatory walkthroughs read paternalistic; minimize and offer skip), Ruler (power users want raw control, not training wheels). For Outlaw / Ruler archetype apps, lean coach marks (item 22) over walkthroughs.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against 'Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload' (Six-Antipattern #1) when a walkthrough is replaced with a card carousel. Pairs against 'No-Escape Room' (#3) when walkthrough is mandatory. Anti-pairs with stubbed-simulation antipattern.
sources: [Strategic Architecture and Psychological Frameworks for Mobile Application Onboarding (project PDF — Pudding Monsters cite)](.agent/Deep%20Dive%20Into%20App%20Onboarding.pdf)
[Anna Anthropy / Daniel Cook — Game Design as Learning Theory](http://www.lostgarden.com/2007/11/games-as-art-creating-meaning.html)
[Koedinger et al. (2012) — Learning gains from interactive vs passive instruction (PNAS)](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1117465109)

uncertain: specific A/B test conversion-lift figures for mobile walkthroughs (gaming-industry data is internal benchmarks)
